title: Understanding Dog Knee Clicking: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's knee clicking? Learn about common causes like luxating patella, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with the right information.
content_html:
TL;DR: A clicking knee is typically caused by a luxating patella or ligament injury and is rarely an emergency unless your dog is in severe pain or unable to bear weight. Capturing a video of your dog walking can significantly help your veterinarian diagnose the cause during a scheduled appointment.

What is causing the clicking or popping sound in my dog's knee?

A clicking or popping sound coming from a dog's knee while walking is often related to mechanical movement within the joint. The most common causes include a luxating patella, where the kneecap slips out of its groove, or a tear in the cranial cruciate ligament (CCL). In the case of a CCL injury, the clicking often indicates that the meniscus—the cushioning cartilage in the knee—is being pinched or has been damaged due to joint instability.

Is a clicking knee in my dog a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a clicking knee is generally Low and is rarely a life-threatening emergency.
  • Monitor your dog for signs of significant distress, such as being completely unable to put weight on the leg or vocalizing in pain.
  • If your dog is otherwise acting normally, you can typically wait to schedule a regular appointment with your veterinarian during business hours.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog's condition?

  • Capturing a video allows a veterinary professional to see the frequency of the click and identify which phase of the stride triggers the sound.
  • Video footage helps the vet assess the degree of lameness.
  • A clear photo of the knee area while your dog is standing can reveal swelling or postural changes that help determine the right priority for care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A clicking sound during locomotion or flexion and extension of the stifle joint may indicate medial meniscal injury. Palpation of the stifle joint may reveal patellar displacement, indicative of patellar luxation. Radiography can reveal joint effusion and signs of degenerative joint disease in chronic injuries. Assessment should include a tibial compression test (flexion of the hock and cranial displacement of the tibial tuberosity) to evaluate cranial cruciate ligament laxity, and palpation to assess the patella's position. The severity of patellar luxation is graded (I-IV) based on clinical signs, frequency of luxation, and presence of limb deformities.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Is my dog in pain if their knee is clicking?
faqs.answer: Not always. Some dogs with mild luxating patellas experience no pain, while others with meniscus tears can be quite uncomfortable. Changes in behavior or activity levels are the best indicators of pain.

faqs.question: Should I stop exercising my dog until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: It is best to limit activity to short, controlled leash walks for bathroom breaks. Avoid jumping, running, or stairs until a veterinarian has performed an orthopedic exam.

faqs.question: Can supplements fix a clicking knee?
faqs.answer: While joint supplements like glucosamine and chondroitin support overall joint health, they cannot mechanically fix a slipping kneecap or a torn ligament.
